segments.s702-g33: وزان is a compressed classical phrase (= وز + آن) meaning 'and/from that (side)'; روی here means 'side' or 'direction'. The following sequence رومی سواران شاه has the adjective before the noun (رومی modifies سواران) and the possessor after the noun (سوارانِ شاه = 'the king's horsemen'), so the whole segment reads 'the king's Roman horsemen'.
segments.s702-g34: برفتند is a past plural verb 'they went/advanced' (here with a military sense 'advanced'). پویان is a participial/adjectival form 'moving, active' that modifies the subject ('advancing'). ب + دان together form the classical locative/ demonstrative 'بدان' = 'to that/onto that' (here 'toward that'); بارگاه is the object 'royal court'.
segments.s702-g35: ببستند is a past plural verb meaning 'they closed' or 'they drew together' (i.e., closed ranks or took position). بر پیش is a prepositional phrase 'before / in front of' (پیش = 'in the presence of'), and میان here indicates 'in the midst' or 'among/within' the assembly; خسرو is the addressee (Khosrow).
segments.s702-g36: که introduces reported speech or content ('that' / 'saying that'). ما is the pronoun 'we'. جنگ جوییم is a present-tense construction where جوییم (from جستن/جوییدن) means 'we seek' — here 'we seek battle' (جنگ as direct object). زایرانیان follows as a nominative/appositive noun 'the pilgrims', i.e. 'we, the pilgrims'. The order places the predicate before the appositive for emphasis.
segments.s702-g37: بدان is the classical locative/phrasing 'on/with that' (here written as ب + دان in the segmentation) and introduces 'on that matter'. کار is 'matter/affair'. همداستان گشت is a predicate: همداستان 'in agreement/like-minded' and گشت (past of گشتن) 'became'. شاه is the subject 'the king', placed after the verb for stylistic emphasis.
segments.s702-g38: کزو is a relative/adverbial 'from which / whereupon' linking this action to what preceded. آرزو خواست is a two-word predicate meaning 'desired/asked for' (آرزو = desire; خواست = 'he wished/asked' in past). رومی سپاه is the object 'a Roman army' with adjective before noun (رومی modifies سپاه). The understood subject of خواست is the king named in the previous clause.
segments.s702-g1: بیامد is the simple past 3rd-person singular ('came') in Ferdowsi's epic style. The prepositional phrase به نزدیک ('towards/near') follows the verb. چوبینه modifies مرد: here چوبینه is a noun/adjective 'club/club-bearing' describing the man (i.e. 'a man with a club'). Word order is verb — prepositional phrase — noun phrase, typical of older Persian poetic inversion.
segments.s702-g2: شنیده is a perfect participle 'having heard' placed before the noun to form a participial phrase modifying the implied subject. سخنها همه = 'all the words/speeches' (همه quantifies سخنها). یادکرد here functions as an archaic fused verbal phrase meaning 'recalled/remembered' (classical order: participle + object(s) + verb).
segments.s702-g3: چو is the conjunction 'when' introducing a temporal clause. مرد جهانجوی is a noun plus epithet ('the world-seeking/ambitious man'), نامه is the direct object, and بخواند is the 3rd-person past form 'read/recited' in Ferdowsi's register (the same form can serve different verbal senses depending on context).
segments.s702-g4: هوارا is an adverbial/accusative-like phrase 'with a cry' (literally 'a cry' used adverbially). Here بخواند is used as 'called/cried aloud' (not 'read'). The coordinate clause وخرد را براند: و‑ joins the next verb, خرد is the object 'wisdom/reason', را is the definite object marker, and براند is past 3rd-person 'drove away' — together 'and drove away wisdom.'
segments.s702-g5: ازان نامه‌ها = 'from those letters' (ازان = 'from that/of those'). ساز رفتن is an idiomatic combination where ساز ('preparation/impulse' metaphorically) plus the infinitive رفتن ('to go') expresses 'the resolve/impulse to leave.' گرفت is the past 3rd-person 'took'/'seized' (i.e. 'took hold').
segments.s702-g6: بماندند is past 3rd-person plural 'they remained' (verb precedes subject by poetic inversion). ایرانیان is the subject 'the Iranians', and درشگفت is an adverbial phrase 'in astonishment' describing their state. Together: 'The Iranians remained in astonishment.'
segments.s702-g7: برفتند is past 3rd-person plural 'they went' or 'they went toward.' پیران is the subject 'the elders.' به نزدیک is the prepositional phrase 'to/near,' and اوی is the archaic third-person pronoun used here as the object 'him' (thus 'they went near him').
segments.s702-g8: چو combined with دیدند yields 'when they saw' (چو = 'when' + دیدند = 'they saw' fused as a common poetic pattern). کردار تاریک اوی is a noun phrase 'his dark deeds' where کردار ('deed') is modified by the adjective تاریک ('dark/obscure') and finalized by the possessive/pronominal اوی ('his').
segments.s702-g25: 'همه' is the subject ('all/everyone'). 'برنشستند' is past 3rd person plural of برنشستن (literally 'sat upon' = 'mounted') so the clause means 'they mounted/sat upon (their horses)'. 'گردان براسپ' is an appositive/adverbial phrase placed after the verb: گردان (troop) qualified by براسپ ('on horseback/astride'), giving the manner/collective reference of 'all'. Word order is typical poetic inversion: subject + verb, then appositive/adverbial.
segments.s702-g26: This is a nominal phrase listing those mounted: 'یلان' (heroes) followed by the attributive 'سینه' ('of chest' = 'brave‑hearted'), then coordinated with 'و' to 'مهتر ایزد گشسپ' — 'the chief, the divine Goshasp.' 'ایزد' here is an honorific adjective ('divine/Godlike') modifying the proper name گشسپ.
segments.s702-g27: 'بدیدار' is a verbal noun meaning 'to meet/for meeting' functioning adverbially ('in order to meet' or 'to meet'). It governs the following noun phrase 'آن لشکر' ('that army'). 'کینه خواه' is a compound adjective modifying لشکر: 'کینه' (hatred/grudge) + 'خواه' ('seeking/wanting'), together 'seeking vengeance' or 'vengeful.'
segments.s702-g28: گرانمایگان is a poetic plural meaning 'the illustrious/noble ones' (subject). برگرفتند is past 3rd person plural of برگرفتن 'to take up' (here 'took up [the road]' = 'set out'). راه is the direct object 'road/path.' The clause follows a normal Past (S‑V‑O) ordering common in classical Persian poetry.
segments.s702-g29: 'چو' fused with 'لشکر' (written as چولشکر) forms a temporal clause 'when the army...' or 'on seeing the army.' بدیدند is past 3rd person plural 'they saw.' باز آمدند is verb phrase 'they came back/returned' with باز ('back/again') plus آمدند (they came). The sequence gives a temporal → action progression: upon seeing, they returned.
segments.s702-g30: 'به نزدیک' is a prepositional phrase 'to/into the vicinity of' (به + نزدیک). 'مهتر' is the object ('the chief'). 'فراز آمدند' combines فراز (up/on high/forward) with آمدند ('they came') to mean 'they came up/advanced' — a poetic motion verb placing the motion at the end (verb-final). Overall: 'they came up near the chief.'
segments.s702-g31: 'که' is a subordinating conjunction here introducing an explanatory or reported clause ('that/for'). 'این بی‌کرانه' is 'this boundless/limitless (host)', with بی‌کرانه post‑nominal adjective. 'یکی لشکرند' predicates that they are 'one army' — لشکرند is a collective noun with plural verb ending, so the clause reads 'this boundless (mass) is one army.'
segments.s702-g32: 'ز' is the poetic preposition 'from' (meaning 'beyond' here). 'اندیشه ما' = 'our thought/understanding' (اندیشه = thought; ما = our). 'همی‌بگذرند' is present/habitual 3rd person plural with the archaic/prosodic prefix همی‑, meaning 'they pass by/above' (i.e., 'they surpass our thought'). Word order places the verb at the end as usual.
segments.s702-g17: همی‌تاخت is an older/progressive verbal form: همی- marks continuous or repeated action and تاخت is the past/stem 'charge/rush', so 'he was charging / charged on'. تا here expresses extent ('as far as' / 'until') introducing the place that follows; آذر آبادگان is a place-name (آذر as proper name + آبادگان 'settlement(s)'). The whole chunk means 'he charged on to Azarabadgan.'
segments.s702-g18: This is a noun phrase describing the force: سپاهی (host/army) followed by the post‑nominal modifier دلاور ('brave/valiant'), then the origin/description introduced by poetic ز ('from'). آزادگان is a plural noun 'the free' (i.e. free/noble men). Word order (noun + adjective + ز + origin) is typical in older Persian.
segments.s702-g19: سپاه اندر آمد is literal 'the host came in/entered', with اندر an archaic preposition 'in/into'. بتنگ سپاه repeats سپاه as predicate with بتنگ ('narrow, confined'), i.e. 'the host was hemmed in / pressed into a narrow place.' Repetition of the noun as subject and predicate is common in classical style.
segments.s702-g20: ببستند is a past 3rd‑pl. verb 'they closed/blocked'. The preposition بر is used twice in a parallel frame ('بر مور و بر پشه') and governs راه at the end: 'they closed (the) road against/on (such that) even ants and gnats (could not pass)'. This is a vivid, hyperbolic way to say the way was completely shut.
segments.s702-g21: چنین گفت is 'thus said' (چنین = 'thus/so', گفت = 'said' in reported speech). پس here is temporal/sequence 'then'. مهتر is the title 'chief/headman', and کینه‌خواه is a compound meaning 'one who seeks/harbours a grudge (vengeful)'; خواه functions as 'desiring/seeking' in the compound. The phrase names the speaker: 'then the chief, eager for revenge, said...'.
segments.s702-g22: که introduces the content clause 'that ...'. من is the subject 'I'. کرد + خواهم is a light‑verb + auxiliary cluster: کرد (do/make) combines with the future auxiliary خواهم ('I will') to express a future action, and the verbal complement is به لشکر نگاه ('a watch/attention toward the army' — به + لشکر marks the target). So the clause means 'that I will keep watch over the army.'
segments.s702-g23: ببینم is a first‑person subjunctive/jussive often used as 'let me see' or 'may I see'; it introduces an inquiry. که introduces the clause. رومی سواران is the subject 'the Roman (Byzantine) horsemen'. کیند is an archaic 3rd‑person plural copular/verb form (functioning like 'are' / 'they are'). The clause thus asks to identify the Roman horsemen.
segments.s702-g24: This is a paired interrogative: سپاهی کدامند 'which are the troops?' (کدامند = 'which are they?') and گردان کیند repeats the same question for 'گردان' (units/troops) using the older copula کیند. The structure lists two categories to be identified (troops and divisions).
segments.s702-g9: همی‌گفت is a habitual/past-continuous formula meaning "kept saying." هرکس introduces a free relative clause "whoever/anyone who"; کز is the relative particle "who/that." ایدر is an adverb meaning "here/from here." مرو is being used as a place name (Merv) and therefore supplies the destination inside the relative clause; the clause is left open and completed by the next segment's verb phrase.
segments.s702-g10: زرفتن here functions as the verbal notion of 'in/by going' that completes the previous relative clause (archaic/poetic compound: ز + رفتن); کهن is the predicate adjective "old/ancient" describing what will be changed; گردد (third person singular of گشتن/گردیدن) means "becomes" or "will become"; این روز نو is the phrase "this new day/this new time," serving as the result/state expressed after گردد. Together the clause says the act of going will make the old become new on this day.
segments.s702-g11: اگر marks a conditional clause "if." خسرو is the personal name (Khosrow) and the subject of آید; آید (present/subjunctive/optative third person of آمدن) here means "comes/arrives." به is the preposition "into/to," and ایران‌زمین is the compound noun "the land/realm of Iran," the destination of آید.
segments.s702-g12: نبینی is a negative-form present/subjunctive 2nd/3rd-person verb used idiomatically as "you will see" or more literally "you will not see" (here implying 'you will see nothing but...'). مگر functions as "except/only" introducing the exception. گرز و شمشیر are coordinated nouns "maces and swords," and کین is a postpositive noun/adjective meaning "of vengeance/hatred," coloring the weapons as instruments of vengeance. The clause asserts that nothing will be seen except vengeful weapons.
segments.s702-g13: برین is the prepositional phrase formed from بر + the demonstrative/marker -ین, so برین تخت = "on this throne." تخت شاهی literally "throne of kingship/royal throne." مخور is a negative imperative of خوردن and idiomatically means "do not take/eat" i.e. "do not set your heart on / do not enjoy/seek"; زینهار is an interjection/imperative meaning "beware" or "take heed." The whole segment issues a warning not to cling to the royal throne.
segments.s702-g14: همی‌خیره is a durative/perfective descriptive form meaning "kept staring/was amazed." بفریبدت is a verb in the optative/may-form with the enclitic object "-ت" meaning "may (it) deceive you" or "might beguile you." روزگار is the noun the verb refers to — here best rendered as "time/fortune/fate" that, by its appearance, can deceive. The line warns that fortune/time, by its dazzling show, can beguile you.
segments.s702-g15: نیامد here is impersonal/idiomatic meaning "(the) words did not avail / words were of no use." سخنها is the plural "words/speeches." برو کارگر is an older collocation meaning "for usefulness/effective" (برو here functions adverbially, and کارگر means "effective/useful"). The segment says that talk proved ineffective.
segments.s702-g16: بفرمود is a polite/honorific past-tense verb "he commanded/ordered." تا here introduces a purposive/completive clause "so that/that" (i.e. he ordered that ...). رفت is the past of رفتن "went/left," and لشکر is "the army/host." بدر is a proper name/place here (not the literal 'full moon'), so the clause says he ordered and the army then went to Badr.

sentences.s702-s17: From that side the king's Roman horsemen advanced, moving toward that royal court.
sentences.s702-s18: They drew up before Khosrow in the midst and declared, 'We, the pilgrims, seek battle.'
sentences.s702-s19: On that matter the king became like-minded, and he desired a Roman army.
sentences.s702-s1: A man with a wooden club came close; having heard the words, he remembered them all.
sentences.s702-s2: When the world-seeking man read the letter, he cried out and drove away wisdom.
sentences.s702-s3: From those letters an impulse to depart took hold; the Iranians remained in astonishment.
sentences.s702-s4: The elders went up close to him; when they saw his dark deeds...
sentences.s702-s13: All mounted their horses — the troop on horseback: the brave‑chested heroes and the great (divine) chief Goshasp.
sentences.s702-s14: To meet that vengeful army, the illustrious men took up the road.
sentences.s702-s15: When they saw the army, they returned; they came up close to the chief.
sentences.s702-s16: For this boundless multitude is a single army; they pass beyond our thought.
sentences.s702-s9: He charged on as far as Azarabadgan — an army of brave men from the free.
sentences.s702-s10: The host entered and was driven into a narrow place; they blocked the road against ants and gnats.
sentences.s702-s11: Thus said then the chief — the vengeance‑seeker — that 'I will keep watch over the army.'
sentences.s702-s12: Let me see who the Roman horsemen are — which troops they are and which the divisions.
sentences.s702-s5: He kept saying, "Whoever from here goes to Merv, by going will make the old become new this very day."
sentences.s702-s6: If Khosrow comes into the land of Iran, you will see nothing but maces and swords of vengeance.
sentences.s702-s7: Do not covet this throne of kingship—take heed; time, staring, may deceive you.
sentences.s702-s8: Words did not avail; he commanded that the army go forth, and the host departed for Badr.
